1: Third year of Jehoiakim — the dating creates a surface tension with Jeremiah 25:1, which places Nebuchadnezzar's first western campaign in Jehoiakim's fourth year. The resolution lies in counting systems: Daniel follows Babylonian accession-year reckoning (the coronation year counts as year 0, so "year 1" begins at the first New Year after accession), while Jeremiah uses Judean non-accession reckoning (the coronation year itself is "year 1"). The two systems yield the same regnal year labelled differently by one integer. Nebukadretssar (H5019) is a Hebrew approximation of Akkadian Nabû-kudurri-uṣur ("Nebo protect the boundary-stone").
2: The Lord gave (Adonai, H136) — Daniel's narrator uses Adonai here rather than the covenant name YHWH. Daniel avoids YHWH throughout, reflecting the theological reticence of exile: the covenant Name belongs to the Land and Temple cult; in Babylon, the more universal titles Adonai and Elohim predominate. Elohim (H430) refers to both the temple of Israel's God and the house of "his god" (Marduk) in the same verse — a deliberate irony. Treasuries (otsar, H214) — the temple vessels ending in Babylon's treasury foreshadow the central crisis of Daniel 5.
3: Seed (zera, H2233) — a covenantal term normally used for offspring of the promise (Abraham, David, the holy seed). These young men are the covenant seed of Israel being absorbed into empire. Saris (H5631) may mean "eunuch" (literal) or simply "court official" (extended); the context suggests both roles existed, with the intent being social as well as physical reorientation.
4: Wisdom vocabulary triad: chokmah (H2451, practical wisdom), da'at (H1847, knowledge/relational knowing), and bin (H995, discernment/understanding) — the three epistemic terms of Proverbs' instruction. Babylon is consciously recruiting Israel's covenant-wisdom tradition for its own service. Ability (koach, H3581) implies physical and intellectual strength; these are Israel's finest, selected to represent the empire's cosmopolitan competence.
5: Daily portion (pathbag, H6598) — a Persian loanword, appearing only in Daniel and Esther in the Hebrew Bible, meaning the royal allotment of food and wine. The issue Daniel refuses is not merely dietary hygiene but covenantal boundary: food from the king's table was likely offered to Babylonian deities, making consumption an act of participation in Babylonian cult. Gadel (H1431, to rear/nourish) uses the same root as "great" — Babylon's program of greatness-formation is enacted through food. Three years — H7969 shaloshet; a fixed apprenticeship period, after which imperial service begins.
6: Four Hebrew theophoric names: Daniel (H1840, "El is my judge"), Hananiah (H2608, "YHWH has been gracious"), Mishael (H4332, "who is what El is?"), Azariah (H5838, "YHWH has helped"). All four embed Israel's covenant God; all four will be renamed. The tribe of Judah (H3063) is the messianic tribe — this detail is theologically loaded given later Davidic echoes.
7: Name replacement — the Babylonian names (Belteshazzar, Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego) embed Babylonian deity names (Bel, Aku/Marduk). The renaming is an act of identity colonization: to rename is to reclaim ownership, erasing the theophoric Hebrew names and their testimony to YHWH. The four men will demonstrate throughout the book that names do not determine identity.
8: Defile (ga'al, H1351) — to contaminate, make ritually impure. The same root underlies go'el (kinsman-redeemer) by ironic contrast: Daniel refuses what cannot be redeemed. Set upon his heart (sam al-libbo) — the lev (H3820) in Hebrew is the seat of decision and will, not merely sentiment. Daniel's act is a covenantal act of will, not just conscientious scruple. Sought permission (vayevaqesh, H1245) — the deliberate, courteous approach models how covenant faithfulness is exercised within empire: not provocative refusal but principled request.
9: Favour and compassion — chesed (H2617) and rachamim (H7356): the two great covenant-mercy terms appear together. God's response to Daniel's faithful resolve is not passive; God actively works within the bureaucratic structure of the empire to open the way. This pairing recurs in covenantal contexts throughout the OT (Exodus 34:6; Hosea 2:19).
10: Appearance (mar'eh, H4758) — physical countenance as visible evidence of inner state. The official's concern is practical: visible malnourishment would reflect badly on the palace and endanger him. Na'ar (H5288) = young man; the diminutive framing underlines their vulnerability within the imperial system.
11: The meltsar (H4453) — often translated "steward" or "overseer"; the term may be a loanword for a specific court functionary. Daniel goes to the more proximate official rather than forcing the issue with Ashpenaz, a pragmatic but also courteous strategy.
12: Vegetables (zeroa'im, H2235, lit. "things sown" or "seed-vegetables") — the word emphasizes plant-produce as opposed to meat. Water only (mayim, H4325) — the contrast with the wine of the king's table is sharp. The proposal is not asceticism but a practical boundary marker.
13: Compare (u're'eh, from H7200 ra'ah) — "and you will see"; an empirical test of whether faithfulness to covenant norms produces flourishing. The book consistently appeals to visible, verifiable outcomes as evidence of divine blessing.
14: He agreed (vayishma', from H8085 shama) — literally "he heard them." The same term used for obedience to God's voice is used here for the official's response to Daniel's request; hearing and heeding are both shama. The ten-day test period mirrors the ten plagues and other ten-day/ten-unit testing patterns in the OT.
15: Healthier and better nourished — bari (H1277, fat/robust) and besar (H1320, flesh); the physical evidence of covenantal blessing is unmistakable. "Better nourished" than those eating the royal menu subverts the implicit claim that imperial provision is the path to human flourishing.
16: The steward simply acts on what he observed: nasa' (H5375) their food and gave (natan, H5414) vegetables. The matter is resolved quietly and practically — no confrontation, no decree, just the empirical outcome vindicating Daniel's request.
17: Knowledge and wisdom given by God — the triad of ch Daniel 1:4 (chokmah, da'at, bin) is now attributed explicitly to divine gift, not Babylonian education. Chazon (H2377) and chalom (H2472) are distinguished: chazon is prophetic/visionary revelation (the major prophetic genre), while chalom is the ordinary dream vehicle. Daniel receives both. This verse is the theological hinge: whatever Babylon trained them in, God is the source of their extraordinary competence.
18: The miqetz (H7093, at the end of) — a typical Hebrew temporal formula marking the fulfillment of the appointed period. Hevisham (from H935, brought/presented) — the official presents them to Nebuchadnezzar for examination.
19: Stood before the king (vaya'amdu lifnei ha-melek, H5975) — to "stand before" a king is the technical phrase for entering royal service. None were found equal (nimtsa', H4672) — "to be found" is a legal/evaluative term; the four are the standard against which all others are measured.
20: Ten times (eser yadot) — hyperbolic superiority formula. Chartomim (H2748, magicians) and ashshaphim (H826, enchanters/astrologers) — the same two categories Pharaoh summons in Exodus 7:11; the echo is not accidental. Daniel and his companions surpass Babylon's wise men as decisively as Moses surpassed Egypt's.
21: Daniel remained until the first year of Cyrus — a chronological bracket closing Daniel 1. The first year of Cyrus (H3566) = 539 BCE, when the Persians conquered Babylon and Cyrus issued his famous decree permitting exiles to return. Daniel's survival across the entire exile period — from the Babylonian conquest of Jerusalem to the Persian restoration — is itself a covenant-faithfulness witness.
